name: Minor Second
description: The smallest commonly used typographic scale, with very subtle size differences between steps. Creates a calm, restrained hierarchy.
use_case: Dense UIs, data tables, dashboards where space is limited and subtle differentiation is needed

name: Major Second
description: A gentle scale with modest jumps between sizes. Provides clear hierarchy without dramatic contrast between levels.
use_case: Corporate websites, documentation, editorial content requiring clear but understated hierarchy

name: Minor Third
description: A popular, well-balanced scale with noticeable but not jarring size progression. Strikes a good balance between harmony and contrast.
use_case: General-purpose web applications, blogs, marketing sites with moderate visual hierarchy needs

name: Major Third
description: A clearly defined scale with satisfying jumps between levels. Creates confident, legible hierarchy with strong visual rhythm.
use_case: Landing pages, portfolios, marketing materials where strong visual contrast between type sizes is desired

name: Perfect Fourth
description: A dramatic scale that creates clear, bold hierarchical differences between size steps. Derived from the musical perfect fourth interval.
use_case: Editorial design, magazine layouts, hero sections, large-display contexts where bold hierarchy is a design goal

name: Augmented Fourth
description: Based on the square root of 2 (the tritone), this scale creates a mathematically elegant progression with each level roughly 1.414x the previous.
use_case: Design systems requiring mathematical precision, geometric layouts, poster design with large display headings

name: Perfect Fifth
description: A bold and highly contrasted scale producing very large display sizes relative to body text. The perfect fifth is a foundational musical harmonic.
use_case: Hero sections, splash screens, large-format print, presentations where dramatic scale contrast is intentional

name: Golden Ratio
description: Uses phi (φ ≈ 1.618), the golden ratio found throughout nature and classical art. Creates an aesthetically pleasing progression with rapid size growth.
use_case: Artistic and luxury brand websites, design portfolios, contexts where the golden ratio aesthetic is a deliberate design statement

name: Major Sixth
description: An extreme scale derived from the major sixth musical interval. Produces very large headings that dwarf body text, creating stark visual impact.
use_case: Splash pages, high-impact advertising, digital signage, or when a maximum contrast between body and display sizes is required

name: Double Octave
description: Each step doubles the previous size, inspired by the musical octave. Produces extremely dramatic scale contrasts rarely used beyond a few levels.
use_case: Large-format displays, experimental typography, contexts where only two or three size levels are used with maximum contrast
